summary: Gruber dissects Epic CEO Tim Sweeney's argument that game consoles deserve their 30% revenue cut from Fortnite but Apple and Google's app stores do not. Sweeney claims consoles are different because hardware is sold at or below cost and subsidized by software sales, while Apple and Google profit enormously from hardware and ads. Gruber argues this distinction doesn't hold up, noting that Android hardware makers also sell at or below cost, and that Epic's failed attempt to distribute Fortnite outside the Play Store proved Google's store serves a console-like role. He points out that Sweeney's argument amounts to 'Apple makes enough money already,' which is weak unless you're an anti-capitalist. Gruber concludes that if Epic wins against Apple and Google, game consoles would logically be next.
key_points: Sweeney's distinction between console and app store fees rests on hardware subsidization, but Android OEMs also sell hardware at or below cost, undermining the argument
Epic's failed experiment distributing Fortnite outside the Play Store proved Google's store serves an analogous role to console platforms
Sweeney's argument that Apple and Google make enough money elsewhere to not need 30% is essentially an anti-capitalist position from someone who is not an anti-capitalist
Console makers like Sony and Nintendo don't even publicly disclose their 30% commission rates, suggesting they don't want scrutiny of these fees
If Epic wins against Apple and Google, game console platform fees would logically face the same challenge next
money_quotes: You can certainly argue that none of them deserve 30 percent of revenue, or that none of them should allow alternative payment services, but it doesn't hold water to say that Xbox, PlayStation, and Nintendo's rules are fine but Google Play Store's are not.

'Apple makes enough money from hardware and Google makes enough money from ads' does not a compelling argument make, unless you're a pretty hardcore anti-capitalist, and Tim Sweeney does not exactly seem like an anti-capitalist.
It tastes like weak sauce even by the standards of sauce obtained from The Weak Sauce Store.
In the same tweet in which he says 'I don't have a problem with the concept of console platform fees' he explains exactly why he has a problem with console fees.
The pro-consumer argument is perfectly valid, but it applies every bit as much to game consoles as to app stores.
If Epic registers a significant win against Apple and Google in this fight, the game consoles are likely next.
I suggest not holding your breath waiting for Microsoft (let alone Sony or Nintendo) to file a friend of the court brief for Epic.
key_insight: Sweeney's argument that console platform fees are justified but app store fees are not collapses under scrutiny because the economic conditions he cites as distinguishing them actually apply to both.
